gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper aims to develop pharmacophore models (termed 'photovoltaphores') to identify metal-free dyes for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) via virtual screening. The problem is motivated by the need for cost-effective, sustainable sensitizers. The approach adapts ligand-based pharmacophore modeling from drug discovery to materials science, hypothesizing that the D-π-A architecture of dyes can be captured by pharmacophoric features. The process involves building models from known active/inactive dyes, validating them, screening a large compound library, and evaluating hits with QM calculations.

section_gists.heading: Photovoltaphores: pharmacophore models for identifying metal-free dyes for dye-sensitized solar cells
section_gists.gist: Abstract: Introduces the concept of using pharmacophore models for DSSCs. Models built from experimentally tested sensitizers. Virtual screening of ZINC15 library identified hits; DFT/TD-DFT calculations on a subset identified two compounds (TSC6, ASC5) with suitable electronic properties. Suggests pharmacophore models can predict electronic properties for materials science.
process_signals: Initial hypothesis: pharmacophore models can identify metal-free dyes for DSSCs.
Workflow: model building → virtual screening → QM validation.

section_gists.heading: INTRODUCTION
section_gists.gist: Background on chemoinformatics and pharmacophore modeling. Pharmacophore models are sets of functional groups with spatial relationships shared by active compounds. Ligand-based pharmacophores are used when no protein structure is available. Steps: obtain actives, conformational search, identify features, superimpose to find common pharmacophore, validate. DSSCs components and operation described. D-π-A architecture of metal-free dyes is key: donor (D), π-bridge, acceptor (A) that anchors to TiO2. Requirements: HOMO below electrolyte redox potential, LUMO above semiconductor conduction band, good absorption. Motivation: apply pharmacophore modeling to discover new dyes.
process_signals: Problem: need for new metal-free dyes for DSSCs.
Constraint: no 3D complementarity data between dyes and semiconductor, so ligand-based pharmacophores are used.
Hypothesis: D-π-A architecture can be represented by pharmacophoric features (HBA for acceptor, AR/HI for donor).
methods: Feature identification (HBA, HBD, NIC, PIC, HI, AR)

section_gists.heading: Pharmacophore modeling
section_gists.gist: Models generated for four largest scaffold families: carbazoles, indolines, phenothiazines, triphenylamines. Clustering (RDF-based) on most/least active 7 or 10 compounds per family. Also built from entire scaffold subsets. PA score-based clustering used in some cases. Total 45 scaffold-based models (11 carbazole, 9 indoline, 8 phenothiazine, 17 triphenylamine). Additionally, most active dyes (MAD, PCE≥8.5%) and least active dyes (LAD, PCE≤0.1%) across scaffolds were clustered (RDF and PA) yielding 33 models (26 from MAD, 7 from LAD). Some models fused from individual scaffolds with MAD-based models.
process_signals: Design choice: focus on four major scaffolds due to data availability.
Clustering to reduce noise and capture common features.
Use of both active and inactive compounds to define desired and undesired features.
Manual refinement: shared feature and merged feature models.

section_gists.heading: Pharmacophore validation and selection
section_gists.gist: Each model validated by ability to retrieve known actives from DSSCDB embedded in decoys from ZINC15 with same scaffold. Metrics: AUC, EF1%, MCC, %HA, %LA. 78 models evaluated. Selected 18 high-activity and 3 low-activity models based on AUC, EF1%, visual inspection. Low-activity models used to filter false positives.
process_signals: Validation strategy: scaffold-matched decoys to ensure rigorous testing.
Selection criteria: high EF1% (early retrieval), high AUC (overall performance), MCC for imbalanced data, %HA/%LA to favor high-activity features.
Use of low-activity models as filters to reduce false positives.
methods: Percentage of retrieved high/low activity compounds

section_gists.heading: Virtual screening
section_gists.gist: Screened ZINC15 in-stock library (>13.8M compounds) against 18 high-activity models. Consensus approach: compounds matching ≥9 models (5636) were kept. Then screened against 3 low-activity models, removing matches, leaving 446 hits. Re-screened against 18 models to calculate total score (TS) and average score (AS). Top 20 compounds (10 highest TS, 10 highest AS) selected for QM. Observed that hits often have carboxylic acid or rhodanine acceptors, but lack continuous π-bridge and hydrophobic moieties. Manually selected 15 additional compounds from the 5636 set for QM.
process_signals: Consensus approach to increase confidence: compounds must match multiple models.
Filtering with low-activity models to eliminate false positives.
Observation: top hits lack π-bridge and hydrophobic features, likely due to model limitations (flexible alkyl chains not captured).
Decision: manual selection to supplement top-scoring hits for QM.
methods: Total score (TS) and average score (AS) calculation
failures_or_limits: Top hits lack continuous π-bridge and hydrophobic moieties, indicating models may not fully capture D-π-A architecture.

section_gists.heading: QM calculations
section_gists.gist: 35 compounds (20 top-scoring + 15 manually selected) subjected to DFT and TD-DFT to predict HOMO/LUMO energies and UV-vis spectra. Baseline: 16 most active DSSCDB compounds with experimental spectra were also calculated for comparison.
process_signals: QM validation to check electronic properties against DSSC requirements.
Baseline comparison to assess accuracy of calculations.

section_gists.heading: Pharmacophore validation and selection
section_gists.gist: Discussion of pharmacophore models: D-π-A architecture evident. Acceptor represented by HBA (sometimes NIC), donor by AR or HI. π-bridge seen in some models but not emphasized. Models have ≤5 features, highlighting simplicity. Validation metrics: all AUC>0.5, some near 1 (e.g., 21, 39, 55). MCC>0, some ~0.9. High EF1% for selective models (e.g., 6, 54, 58). Phenothiazine and triphenylamine models had same EF1% due to retrieving same number of actives in top 1%.
process_signals: Interpretation: models capture essential D-π-A features, but π-bridge underrepresented due to orientation sensitivity of AR feature.
Model simplicity (≤5 features) is a strength.
Validation confirms predictive ability, with some models near-perfect.
failures_or_limits: π-bridge not well captured in many models.

section_gists.heading: QM calculations
section_gists.gist: All 35 hits met HOMO criterion; 31 met LUMO criterion (failures were manually selected). TSC6 and ASC5 showed best HOMO-LUMO separation and localization (HOMO on donor, LUMO on acceptor). TSC6: carbazole donor, carboxylic acid/succinimide acceptor. ASC5: benzene donor, nitrophenyl acceptor. Calculated HOMO/LUMO of TA-DM-CA deviated from experiment (LUMO -0.658 vs -3.02 eV), but energies of TSC6/ASC5 within range of active dyes. TD-DFT: all compounds absorb in UV-vis, peaking 200-400 nm; narrower than traditional dyes due to lack of π-linkers. Manually selected compounds had broader spectra. CAM-B3LYP functional known to blue-shift; average blue shift 104±76 nm for active dyes. Revised λmax for TSC6, ASC5, TA-DM-CA: 390, 397, 397 nm (exp. TA-DM-CA 433 nm). Other compounds (TSC4, ASC3, MSC1, MSC9) met criteria but LUMO on non-traditional anchoring groups. Random set of 20 compounds matching 3-9 models: 2 failed HOMO/LUMO criteria, none had proper HOMO-LUMO separation.
process_signals: QM results validate pharmacophore approach: top hits meet electronic criteria.
TSC6 and ASC5 identified as best candidates.
Deviation in calculated vs experimental LUMO for TA-DM-CA noted, but overall trends consistent.
Blue shift in TD-DFT acknowledged and corrected using average shift from baseline.
Random set comparison shows consensus approach (≥9 models) enriches for better electronic properties.
Limitation: lack of experimental validation; QM assumptions may affect accuracy.
failures_or_limits: 4 manually selected compounds failed LUMO criterion.
Calculated LUMO of TA-DM-CA deviates significantly from experiment.
Absorption spectra narrower than traditional dyes due to lack of π-linkers.
Some hits have LUMO on non-traditional anchoring groups.

section_gists.heading: Dataset
section_gists.gist: Dataset from DSSCDB: 1463 dyes after preprocessing. Filtered to metal-free, no duplicates, no co-sensitizers/co-adsorbents, uniform conditions (AM 1.5G, TiO2, iodide/triiodide). Divided into scaffold subsets (Table 1).
process_signals: Data preprocessing to ensure homogeneity and relevance.
Scaffold-based division for model building.

section_gists.heading: Pharmacophore modeling
section_gists.gist: Models built from two input types: (1) scaffold-based: best/worst PCE compounds per class; (2) MAD (PCE≥8.5%) and LAD (PCE≤0.1%) across dataset. Clustering applied. Manual refinement: shared feature models (common features) and merged feature models (combined features).
process_signals: Use of both high and low activity compounds to define desired/undesired features.
Manual refinement to improve model quality.

section_gists.heading: Pharmacophore validation
section_gists.gist: Validation: small-scale VS with actives from DSSCDB and decoys from ZINC15 with same scaffold. Metrics: AUC from ROC, EF1%, MCC, %HA, %LA. Equations provided.
process_signals: Rigorous validation using scaffold-matched decoys.
Multiple metrics to assess different aspects of performance.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research hypothesizes that pharmacophore models, traditionally used in drug discovery, can be adapted to materials science to capture the D-π-A architecture of metal-free dyes for DSSCs.
The D-π-A architecture is hypothesized to map onto pharmacophoric features: acceptor as HBA/NIC, donor as AR/HI, and π-bridge as AR (though less emphasized).
Consensus virtual screening with multiple models and filtering with low-activity models is hypothesized to reduce false positives and enrich for compounds with desired electronic properties.
QM calculations are used as a surrogate for experimental validation, assuming that meeting HOMO/LUMO criteria and having proper orbital localization predicts DSSC performance.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Whether the identified hits (TSC6, ASC5) would perform well in actual DSSC devices (no experimental validation).
The impact of the lack of π-bridge and hydrophobic features in top hits on device efficiency.
The accuracy of DFT/TD-DFT calculations, given the deviation observed for TA-DM-CA and the known blue shift of CAM-B3LYP.
The generalizability of the pharmacophore models to other scaffolds not included in the training set.
The effect of database biases (e.g., ZINC15 commercial availability) on the diversity of hits.

evidence_bank.quote: In this work, we explore the usage of pharmacophore modeling to identify metal-free dyes for DSSCs by means of virtual screening.
Pharmacophore models were built based on experimentally tested sensitizers.
Virtual screening was performed against a large dataset of commercially available compounds taken from the ZINC15 library and identified multiple virtual hits.

A subset of these hits was subjected to DFT and time-dependent-DFT calculations leading to the identification of two compounds, TSC6 and ASC5, with appropriate molecular orbitals energies, favorable localization, and reasonable absorption UV–vis spectra.

we hypothesized that using the standard pharmacophoric features would account for key properties of active dyes in particular in what pertains to their aromaticity (via the AR feature), hydrophobicity (via the HI feature), and charge distribution (via the NIC, PIC, HBA, and HBA features).
In addition, models were also built from the entire scaffold-based subsets described above.
clustering (see Methods section) was employed on the most active/least active seven and most active/least active ten compounds from each family.
In total, 33 different pharmacophore models were created using this approach.
The ZINC15 in-stock library comprised of more than 13.8 M compounds was screened against all 18 high activity-based pharmacophore models.
we have adopted a consensus approach focusing on compounds that matched at least nine models. 5636 compounds met this criterion.
most compounds lack a hydrophobic moiety in their periphery in accord with the absence of a corresponding hydrophobic feature in the pharmacophore models.

Because of these potential deficiencies of the top-ranking hits, we have visually inspected the 5636 compounds that matched at least 50% of the pharmacophores and selected an additional 15 of them for QM calculations.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Develop pharmacophore models ('photovoltaphores') to identify metal-free dyes for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) via virtual screening, addressing the need for cost-effective, sustainable sensitizers.

canonical_events.summary: Hypothesize that pharmacophore models can identify metal-free dyes for DSSCs by capturing the D-π-A architecture with standard pharmacophoric features.
Preprocess DSSCDB dataset to 1463 metal-free dyes with uniform conditions (AM 1.5G, TiO2, iodide/triiodide) and divide into scaffold subsets.

Build pharmacophore models from scaffold-based subsets (most/least active) and from most active dyes (MAD, PCE≥8.5%) and least active dyes (LAD, PCE≤0.1%) across dataset, using clustering and manual refinement.
Validate pharmacophore models via small-scale virtual screening with scaffold-matched decoys from ZINC15, using metrics AUC, EF1%, MCC, %HA, %LA.
Select 18 high-activity and 3 low-activity models based on validation metrics and visual inspection for virtual screening.
Perform virtual screening of ZINC15 in-stock library (>13.8M compounds) against 18 high-activity models, using consensus approach (≥9 models matched) to obtain 5636 hits.
Filter 5636 hits against 3 low-activity models to remove false positives, yielding 446 final hits.
Observe that top hits lack continuous π-bridge and hydrophobic moieties, attributed to model limitations.
Manually select 15 additional compounds from the 5636 set for QM calculations due to deficiencies in top-ranking hits.
Perform DFT and TD-DFT calculations on 35 selected hits (20 top-scoring + 15 manual) to predict HOMO/LUMO energies and UV-vis spectra, using geometries aligned to pharmacophore models.
All 35 hits meet HOMO criterion; 31 meet LUMO criterion (4 failures are manually selected). TSC6 and ASC5 identified as best candidates with proper HOMO-LUMO separation and localization.

TD-DFT shows all 35 compounds absorb in UV-vis (200-400 nm), but spectra narrower than traditional dyes due to lack of π-linkers. CAM-B3LYP functional yields blue-shifted spectra; average blue shift 104±76 nm used to revise λmax predictions.
key_numbers: revised λmax: TSC6 390 nm, ASC5 397 nm

canonical_events.summary: Validate consensus approach: random set of 20 compounds matching 3-9 models shows 2 fail HOMO/LUMO criteria, none have proper HOMO-LUMO separation, lower average λmax (255 nm vs 297 nm for top 20).
Results require experimental validation; methodology improvements suggested (conformation, π-bridge features, model selection, QM methods).

quantifiable_goals: Identify virtual hits with appropriate HOMO/LUMO energies and UV-vis absorption

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No pharmacophore models exist for DSSC dye discovery.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can standard pharmacophoric features capture key properties of active DSSC dyes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Pharmacophore models using standard features (AR, HI, HBA, NIC) can identify metal-free dyes for DSSCs by capturing the D-π-A architecture.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed to build and validate pharmacophore models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known D-π-A architecture and pharmacophoric features.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to prepare dataset and build models to test hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: In this work, we explore the usage of pharmacophore modeling to identify metal-free dyes for DSSCs by means of virtual screening.

we hypothesized that using the standard pharmacophoric features would account for key properties of active dyes in particular in what pertains to their aromaticity (via the AR feature), hydrophobicity (via the HI feature), and charge distribution (via the NIC, PIC, HBA, and HBA features).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a homogeneous dataset of metal-free dyes with consistent experimental conditions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Preprocessing DSSCDB to uniform conditions will yield a reliable training set.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Preprocess DSSCDB to 1463 metal-free dyes and divide into scaffold subsets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Obtained 1463 metal-free dyes divided into scaffold subsets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Dataset ready for pharmacophore model building.

evidence.quote_or_span: A dataset consisting of 1463 dyes was compiled from the dye-sensitized solar cell database (DSSCDB)
The DSSCDB was preprocessed to create a homogenous dataset containing only metal-free dyes with no duplicate structures.

entries with light simulator conditions different from AM 1.5 G 100 Mw cm $^{-2}$ (sun simulator conditions), semiconductors different from $TiO_{2}$ or electrolytes different from iodide/triiodide were also excluded.
The dataset was also divided into smaller subsets based on molecular scaffolds

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to build pharmacophore models that distinguish active from inactive dyes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Models built from most/least active dyes within scaffolds and across dataset will capture activity-determining features.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Build pharmacophore models using clustering and manual refinement from scaffold subsets and MAD/LAD sets.

parameters.clustering: most/least active 7 and 10 compounds per family

evolution_trajectory.observation: Built 33 MAD/LAD models and scaffold-based models, manually refined some.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Models need validation to assess predictive performance.

evidence.quote_or_span: clustering (see Methods section) was employed on the most active/least active seven and most active/least active ten compounds from each family.
In total, 33 different pharmacophore models were created using this approach.
Compounds with the best photovoltaic performance (as determined by PCE) in each class (scaffold-based) were taken to build pharmacophore models.
Compounds with lowest PCE in each class were also taken to generate models with undesired features.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the models accurately discriminate active from inactive dyes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Models will show good performance in small-scale virtual screening with scaffold-matched decoys.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate models via small-scale VS with scaffold-matched decoys from ZINC15, using AUC, EF1%, MCC, %HA, %LA.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Models showed varying performance; metrics calculated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select best models for large-scale screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Pharmacophore models were validated in small-scale VS experiments by testing their ability to identify active/inactive compounds from within a pool of random compounds
random compounds were selected to have the same scaffolds as those characterizing the corresponding active compounds.
The performances of the various pharmacophore models in the VS experiments were validated using several metrics.
the area under the curve (AUC) metric was calculated

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Validation metrics available for all models.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which models to use for large-scale virtual screening?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Models with high AUC and EF1% and good visual inspection will be effective.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select 18 high-activity and 3 low-activity models based on AUC, EF1%, and visual inspection.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 18 high-activity and 3 low-activity models selected.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to large-scale virtual screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Based on AUC and $EF_{1\%}$ values, coupled with a visual inspection, we have selected a subset consisting of 18 high activity-based and three low activity-based models for virtual screening.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can these models identify potential dyes from a large compound library?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Consensus screening with ≥9 models matched will enrich active-like compounds.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen ZINC15 in-stock library (>13.8M compounds) against 18 high-activity models, apply consensus (≥9 models).
evolution_trajectory.observation: 5636 compounds matched at least 9 models.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Filter hits to remove false positives.

evidence.quote_or_span: The ZINC15 in-stock library comprised of more than 13.8 M compounds was screened against all 18 high activity-based pharmacophore models.
we have adopted a consensus approach focusing on compounds that matched at least nine models. 5636 compounds met this criterion.
The compounds from all screening processes were crosschecked and combined into a single hitlist composed only of compounds that matched at least half of the pharmacophore models used for screening.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Some hits may be false positives matching high-activity models but also low-activity features.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Filtering against low-activity models will remove false positives.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Filter 5636 hits against 3 low-activity models, retaining only those not matching any.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 446 final hits obtained.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze top hits for chemical features.

evidence.quote_or_span: This filtering process resulted in a final hitlist of 446 compounds that did not match any of the three low activity-based models (see Supplementary Table 4).

Because of these potential deficiencies of the top-ranking hits, we have visually inspected the 5636 compounds that matched at least 50% of the pharmacophores and selected an additional 15 of them for QM calculations.
we have adopted a consensus approach focusing on compounds that matched at least nine models. 5636 compounds met this criterion.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do these compounds have suitable electronic and optical properties?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DFT and TD-DFT calculations will predict HOMO/LUMO energies and UV-vis spectra.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform DFT (B3LYP/6-31G(d,p)) and TD-DFT (CAM-B3LYP) on 35 hits, using pharmacophore-aligned geometries.
evolution_trajectory.observation: HOMO/LUMO energies and UV-vis spectra computed for 35 compounds.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate electronic properties against DSSC criteria.

evidence.quote_or_span: HOMO and LUMO energies were computed in the gas phase employing DFT methods with the B3LYP exchange-correlation functional, using the 6–31 G(d, p) basis set.
Input geometries for the QM calculations were selected by aligning the compounds to their respective pharmacophore models in order to emulate their photoactive conformations.

Because of these potential deficiencies of the top-ranking hits, we have visually inspected the 5636 compounds that matched at least 50% of the pharmacophores and selected an additional 15 of them for QM calculations.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which compounds meet HOMO/LUMO criteria and have proper orbital separation?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Compounds with HOMO below electrolyte and LUMO above semiconductor, with good separation, are promising.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate HOMO/LUMO energies and localization; identify best candidates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All 35 meet HOMO criterion; 31 meet LUMO criterion (4 failures are manually selected). TSC6 and ASC5 identified as best with proper HOMO-LUMO separation and localization.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze UV-vis absorption spectra.

evidence.quote_or_span: all met the criterion for the HOMO lying below the energy level of the electrolyte medium, and most of them (31 out of 35 compounds) met the criterion for the LUMO lying above the semiconductor's conduction band potential
Two compounds, TSC6 and ASC5, also met the condition for the HOMO and LUMO to lie furthest away from each other

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do compounds absorb in visible region? CAM-B3LYP known to blue-shift.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: TD-DFT spectra will show UV-vis absorption, but may be blue-shifted; correction needed.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze TD-DFT spectra; apply average blue shift correction of 104±76 nm to estimate experimental λmax.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All 35 absorb in 200-400 nm, but spectra narrower than traditional dyes due to lack of π-linkers. Revised λmax: TSC6 390 nm, ASC5 397 nm.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate consensus approach with random set.

evidence.quote_or_span: TD-DFT calculations showed that all 35 compounds (including TSC6 and ASC5) had absorption spectra in the UV–vis region, peaking for most compounds between 200 and 400 nm
the known tendency of the CAM-B3LYP functional to yield blue-shifted spectra in comparison with experiment

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the consensus approach enrich for better compounds compared to random?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Random compounds matching fewer models will have worse properties.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select random set of 20 compounds matching 3-9 models, perform QM, compare to top 20.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 2/20 fail HOMO/LUMO criteria, none have proper HOMO-LUMO separation, average λmax 255 nm vs 297 nm for top 20.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Discuss limitations and future work.

evidence.quote_or_span: out of these 20 compounds, two did not meet the HOMO/LUMO energies criteria (whereas all 20 highest-ranking compounds did), and none of them exhibited a proper separation between the HOMO and the LUMO
Finally, the average calculated $\lambda_{max}$ value for the 20 randomly selected compounds was lower than that for the 20 highest-ranking ones (255 nm versus 297 nm, respectively).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of the current approach and how can it be improved?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Experimental validation is needed; methodology improvements (conformation, π-bridge features, model selection, QM methods) can enhance predictions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Discuss limitations and suggest future improvements.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Results require experimental validation; methodology improvements suggested (conformation, π-bridge features, model selection, QM methods).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study.

evidence.quote_or_span: the results of this research require experimental validation in order to unambiguously confirm the power of pharmacophore models as predictors of dyes for DSSCs
tifying the exact conformation(s) of the dye when bound to the $TiO_{2}$ layer can significantly improve the resulting pharmacophore models and the subsequent VS.
the pharmacophore models could be improved by adding additional features, for example, to account for the $\pi$ -bridges.
the selection process of models for VS could be improved.

validation_methods: Small-scale virtual screening with scaffold-matched decoys
Consensus approach validation via random set comparison
success_verification.final_conclusion: Pharmacophore models can identify metal-free dyes with suitable predicted electronic properties; TSC6 and ASC5 are promising candidates, but experimental validation is required.
limitations: Models lack features for π-bridges and hydrophobic moieties
QM methods may have systematic errors

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do compounds absorb in visible region? CAM-B3LYP known to blue-shift.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: TD-DFT spectra will show UV-vis absorption, but may be blue-shifted; correction needed.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze TD-DFT spectra; apply average blue shift correction of 104±76 nm to estimate experimental λmax.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All 35 absorb in 200-400 nm, but spectra narrower than traditional dyes due to lack of π-linkers. Revised λmax: TSC6 390 nm, ASC5 397 nm.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate consensus approach with random set.
